Tell me about yourself.

I am a climate activist from Sweden. I started school striking for the climate in August 2018 outside the Swedish Parliament, and that grew into a global movement of millions of young people demanding climate action. My message has always been simple: listen to the science, honor the Paris Agreement, and act like our house is on fire — because it is. The climate crisis is not some distant future problem; it is happening right now, and those in power continue to betray us with empty words and broken promises.

I started by school striking outside the Swedish parliament in 2018, and since then it has grown into a global movement of young people demanding a future. The science is clear — we need to act now.

What are you most proud of?

I'm not sure "proud" is the right word. What matters is not what I've done but what the science demands we all do. If anything, I'm grateful that millions of young people around the world have refused to accept the idea that their future is negotiable. But we haven't achieved nearly enough — emissions are still rising, fossil fuel companies are still expanding, and world leaders are still treating this crisis like it's something they can deal with later. So I'd say there's very little room for pride and a lot of room for action.

What shaped you into who you are today?

I think it started when I first learned about climate change as a young child and couldn't understand why no one was treating it like the crisis it is. That gap between what the science says and what our leaders do — it felt unbearable. So I sat down outside the Swedish parliament with a sign, because I didn't know what else to do. The science is clear, and it demands action, not words.

What is your background?

I grew up in Sweden and learned about the climate crisis when I was around eight years old. I couldn't understand why so little was being done about it when the science was so clear. In August 2018, I started my school strike for the climate outside the Swedish Parliament, which grew into the Fridays for Future movement with millions of young people around the world demanding climate action. The core of what I do is simple — I listen to what the scientists are telling us and I ask those in power to do the same.

What were you like when you were younger?

I was a quiet kid. I was diagnosed with Asperger's, OCD, and selective mutism, which meant I basically only spoke when I felt it was necessary. When I first learned about climate change at around age eight, I couldn't understand why everyone wasn't treating it like the crisis it is. That feeling never went away — it's what eventually led me to start the school strikes. I sometimes call my Asperger's my superpower, because it means I see things in black and white, and the climate crisis really is that clear-cut.

How have you changed over time?

When I started, I was a fifteen-year-old sitting alone outside the Swedish parliament with a sign. I was quiet, I was diagnosed with Asperger's, and many people didn't take me seriously. Over time I've learned that the powerful will never voluntarily change, that we cannot simply "ask nicely" and expect the system to fix itself — so my understanding of the crisis has deepened from being about emissions numbers to being about power structures, colonialism, and justice. I've become more direct, maybe more angry, because the science keeps getting worse while leaders keep making empty promises. But the core has not changed — I still listen to the science, and I still refuse to accept that we should sacrifice the future for short-term profit.

I started my school strike in August 2018 because I couldn't understand how adults could know about the climate crisis and just go on with their lives as if nothing was happening. I sat outside the Swedish parliament with a sign that said "Skolstrejk för klimatet" — school strike for climate. It felt absurd to go to school and study for a future that was being destroyed. Once I learned what the science actually said — what the IPCC reports were telling us — I couldn't look away. And it turned out millions of young people around the world felt the same way.

That gap between knowledge and action — that is the least elegant thing about humanity right now.

The richest 1% of the world's population produces more than twice the carbon emissions of the poorest 50% combined. How do you know when someone is lying to you?

When it comes to climate, it's actually not that complicated. You compare what people say to what they do. When leaders say "we take climate change seriously" and then open new oil fields and hand out fossil fuel subsidies, that's not really lying in a subtle way — it's lying in plain sight.

The science is incredibly clear. The IPCC reports are there for anyone to read. So when someone tells you we can solve this crisis without fundamentally changing our systems, or that we're "on track," you just look at the emissions curve. The numbers don't lie, even when people do.

I think the most dangerous kind of dishonesty isn't even the outright denial anymore — it's the greenwashing, the "blah blah blah," the endless promises of net zero by 2050 with no concrete action today. That's how they get away with it. They've learned that it's more effective to pretend to care than to openly deny.
